opp.ais_why:
• Intake is in scope as part of S2C with requisitions, but it is framed as scope rather than buyer-voice AI excitement.
• Description is empty and there are no Avoma calls. Engagement is RFI/RFP-driven, with the RFP expected back in the fall.
• Qualified, $120K. Vendor selection by EOY 2026 and 2027 implementation. No urgency or AI mandate signaled.

contacts.name: Tammy Clark
contacts.title: Category Manager - Travel & Expense, Fleet, Professional Services

sources: Tammy Clark, intro call 02 March 2026
Matt Palka, intro call 02 March 2026
metrics.narrative: Buyer articulated the business problem clearly: they need a unified Source-to-Pay platform with embedded supplier risk management to avoid running two separate RFPs and implementations. Tammy stated on the 02 March intro call, 'Ideally, we'll find suppliers that have both, and we won't need to do two giant RFPs and implement two tools.' She also described a data consolidation pain: 'We wanna have all of our data in one place... we don't have all that data in one place. We're not seeing the leakage, and we're not seeing our total spend with the supplier' (02 March call). The buyer currently tracks spend across three silos (S4 for PO, T&E system, credit card data) and lacks unified supplier spend visibility. However, no quantified savings target, cost-per-process reduction, headcount efficiency gain, or ROI has been stated. The pain is confirmed and operational (manual RFP process, homegrown tool limitations, duplicate vendor records in SAP, fragmented spend data), but not yet translated into a dollar or efficiency metric. The move is to run a value discovery session ahead of the RFP to quantify cycle-time savings, Maverick spend reduction, and contract compliance lift, anchoring those to their stated end-of-year selection goal.

sources: OpportunityContactRole: Tammy Clark (Evaluator), Matt Palka (Evaluator)
Next_Step__c: Tammy reached out 01 May asking for pricing; pricing call 07 May; Horizon sent 20 May
Intro call 02 March 2026, pricing call 07 May 2026
Last buyer engagement 07 May (50 days ago), rep outreach 15 June (no response yet)
champion.narrative: Tammy Clark (Category Manager, Travel & Expense, Fleet, Professional Services) is the primary contact and is actively managing the evaluation, but she is not yet a champion in the full sense. She reached out to Bailey on 01 May asking for pricing (per Next_Step__c), engaged on the pricing call 07 May, and has been responsive to RFI submissions and follow-ups. She requested the RFP template, Gartner report, and Horizon event invite, and Bailey sent those materials (per Next_Step__c 08 May and 20 May). Matt Palka (Manager, Indirect Procurement) is the second evaluator and was active on the intro call, clarifying scope and integration requirements. Both are engaged and cooperative, but there is no evidence yet of advocacy, internal selling, or access to the Economic Buyer or senior leadership. Tammy has not shared competitive intel, introduced Zycus to IT or finance stakeholders, or pushed back on competitors. The relationship is transactional and procurement-led (normal for this stage), not yet a coaching or championing dynamic. Since the pricing call on 07 May (50 days ago), there has been no buyer-initiated contact; Bailey reached out 15 June with no response yet. The champion strength is developing because Tammy and Matt are engaged and cooperative, but they have not escalated Zycus internally, provided insider guidance, or opened access to power. The risk is single-threading and cooling engagement. The moves are to (1) invite Tammy and Matt to Horizon (already done 20 May) and use that as a re-engagement and relationship-deepening lever, (2) ask them directly for intros to IT and finance stakeholders to multi-thread, and (3) provide value-add content (e.g., a peer reference in their industry, a case study on S2P+Risk consolidation) to give them ammunition to advocate internally.

sources: Tammy Clark: 'we've talked to a number of suppliers', intro call 02 March 2026
Competitors__c: null
Next_Step__c, Tasks, Avoma calls: no competitor named
competition.narrative: No competitor has been named in any source to date. Competitors__c is empty, no rival mentioned on either call, no competitive intel in Next_Step__c or task history, and no vendor names in the buyer's stated evaluation process. Tammy mentioned they are 'talking to a number of suppliers' on the intro call (02 March), confirming this is a competitive multi-vendor RFI/RFP process, but she did not name any. The RFI was issued in mid-March (per Next_Step__c '3/27: RFI came in this week') and submitted 14 April, so other vendors have also responded. The buyer's stated selection timeline (vendor selected by end of calendar year 2026) and the fall RFP issuance indicate the competitive field will narrow through the RFP round. The lack of named competitors at this early stage is normal (procurement typically does not volunteer the shortlist during intro calls), but it is a knowledge gap that increases risk. The wedge for Zycus is the unified S2P+Risk platform (avoiding two RFPs), the AI/Merlin narrative (autonomous negotiation agents, contract AI review, spend analysis), the SAP integration depth (bi-directional sync, catalog push, deduplication), and the modular pricing flexibility. The move is to ask Tammy and Matt directly on the next call, 'Who else are you evaluating, and how are you thinking about the trade-offs?' to surface the competitive landscape and position Zycus's strengths against the known field. Also recommend running a competitive review with the deal owner's manager before the RFP response to anticipate likely rivals (Coupa, SAP Ariba, Jaggaer, GEP) and prep the differentiation narrative (Merlin AI, risk embedded, modular pricing).

sources: Tammy Clark, intro call 02 March 2026
Matt Palka, intro call 02 March 2026
identify_pain.narrative: The buyer clearly articulated multiple operational pains on the intro call. First, they are running manual RFP/RFQ processes with no automation: Tammy stated, 'We don't have a tool for our RFXs. We do all of that manually' (02 March call). Second, they have a homegrown tool for contract repository and expiration tracking, and Matt described it as limited: 'We have a homegrown tool for document repository, for intake forms... the system basically sends you reminders every 36 to 90 days. Right? So that you don't have to remember when something's coming for renewal' (02 March call). Third, spend data is fragmented across three systems (SAP S4 for PO, T&E system, credit card data), and Tammy owns the pain: 'We wanna have all of our data in one place... we're not seeing the leakage, and we're not seeing our total spend with the supplier' (02 March call). Fourth, SAP has duplicate vendor records that are hard to clean, and Tammy noted Zycus's deduplication capability as a fit. Fifth, they want to avoid running two separate RFPs (one for S2P, one for supplier risk), which would double the procurement and implementation effort: 'Ideally, we'll find suppliers that have both, and we won't need to do two giant RFPs and implement two tools' (02 March call). The pain owner is procurement (Tammy and Matt), and the pain is confirmed and operational, not inferred. The pain is directly tied to the modules in scope (eSourcing, contract management, supplier information, risk, spend analysis), and Zycus demonstrated fit on every point during the intro call. The gap is quantification: no dollar cost of manual RFP cycle time, no error rate or contract compliance metric, no Maverick spend leakage percentage. The move is to run a value discovery session to quantify the pain and build the business case before the RFP.

sources: Tammy Clark NDA request, intro call 02 March 2026
Next_Step__c: RFI submitted 14 April 2026 (implies NDA executed)

Account-level contacts: Peter Osella (VP Reg/Quality/Compliance), Eric Hoberg (Senior Director Internal Audit & Risk)
paper_process.narrative: Formal contracting mechanics are partially visible. Tammy requested an NDA on the intro call (02 March) and asked for Zycus's legal name and headquarters address, indicating a standard procurement paper process. Bailey committed to sending the NDA details, and the NDA was presumably executed (RFI was submitted 14 April, suggesting the NDA gate was cleared). Matt confirmed SAP integration scope, implying an IT/systems engineering review will be required before go-live. The account-level contact list includes InfoSec-adjacent roles (Peter Osella, VP Regulatory/Quality and Chief Compliance Officer; Eric Hoberg, Senior Director Internal Audit & Risk Management), but no InfoSec review or timeline has been stated. No mention of MSA, SOW, or Order Form redlining process, legal review cycle time, or procurement channel (direct vs partner). The buyer stated implementation start 'late this year or early next year' (02 March call), but the contract-to-implementation handoff is not defined. Current_Contract_Expiration__c is not populated, so no incumbent replacement forcing function. The gap is the formal contracting timeline, legal review process, InfoSec questionnaire/review gate, and whether an SI or partner is involved in implementation. The move is to ask Tammy and Matt for the standard contract cycle time (RFP award to signature) and to surface the InfoSec and IT review requirements early, ideally before the RFP response, so those can be baked into the timeline and Zycus can prep the security documentation in advance.

sources: OpportunityContactRole: Tammy Clark (Evaluator), Matt Palka (Evaluator)
Tammy Clark budget comment, intro call 02 March 2026
Account-level contacts (not confirmed opp stakeholders): Mandy Felix, Melissa Cameron, Michele Howard
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified across calls, contact roles, or next-step history. Tammy Clark (Category Manager) and Matt Palka (Manager, Indirect Procurement) are both Evaluators per OpportunityContactRole and are running the RFI/RFP process, but neither has stated budget authority or C-level sponsorship. Tammy mentioned on 02 March, 'We have budget. Our fiscal year starts April one. So we do have some budget for a tool. We don't have the budget for two platforms,' indicating budget is allocated but she did not name who controls it or approves the final commercial decision. The account-level contact list includes potential power roles (Mandy Felix, Director Procurement and Operations; Melissa Cameron, Director Corporate Procurement; Michele Howard, Senior Finance Director), but none have appeared on a call or in a contact role for this opportunity. The broader org chart suggests a multi-approval structure (procurement leadership, finance, potentially IT given the SAP integration scope), but the EB and approval chain are unmapped. The immediate move is to ask Tammy and Matt directly who will sign the contract and approve the budget, and to secure an intro to that person before the RFP response is submitted.

decision_process.narrative: The buyer is running a structured, multi-vendor evaluation managed by procurement (Tammy and Matt). The process to date: intro calls (early March), RFI issuance and submission (RFI due mid-March per Next_Step__c '3/27: RFI came in this week. Due in 2 weeks', submitted 14 April), pricing questions (07 May), and an RFP expected in fall 2026. Tammy stated the goal is 'to have a supplier selected by the end of this year' with 'implementation start late in the year or early next year' (02 March call). The approval chain is not yet documented: no confirmation of who approves budget, whether legal/InfoSec gates are formal, or whether IT (given SAP integration scope) has approval authority. The account-level contact list includes IT leadership (Karen Davis, Director Global IT Development; Sangeetha Sambandan, Solution Architect; Kathryn Hofherr, Manager IT Process Governance), finance (Michele Howard, Senior Finance Director; Mizuho Takami, Assistant Director FP&A), and senior procurement (Mandy Felix, Melissa Cameron), but none have joined a call or been named as decision-makers. The intro call included an NDA request, and Tammy asked for Zycus's legal name and HQ address (02 March call), indicating a formal procurement process. The next formal milestone is the RFP in fall 2026, and the selection decision by end of calendar year 2026. The gap is the approval chain and the formal gates (legal, InfoSec, IT sign-off) between RFP response and contract signature. The move is to map the approval process explicitly with Tammy and Matt, and to request intros to IT and finance stakeholders before the RFP is issued.

sources: Tammy Clark requirements list (chat), intro call 02 March 2026
Matt Palka scope clarification, intro call 02 March 2026
Next_Step__c: RFI submitted 14 April 2026
Products__c: iContract;iSource;ML iAnalyze;iSupplier;Merlin;iRisk
decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer has explicitly stated their evaluation criteria across the intro call and the requirements list Tammy shared in chat on 02 March. Core requirements include: (1) unified Source-to-Pay platform with supplier risk management embedded to avoid two RFPs, (2) eSourcing (RFP/RFQ automation), (3) contract lifecycle management with expiration tracking and AI-assisted review, (4) supplier information management with SAP integration and deduplication, (5) supplier risk and performance management with government-verified data plus integration to their Experian subscription, (6) spend analysis with the ability to pull in T&E and credit card data for unified supplier spend visibility, and (7) catalog management with SAP integration (catalogs created in Zycus pushed to SAP for requisition approval flow). The buyer confirmed they are NOT evaluating P2P, invoicing, or AP (those stay in SAP). Matt stated, 'We are not looking at anything related to purchase requisition to PO process... invoicing... or AP perspective that's staying outside of this tool' (02 March call). AI capability and Microsoft Teams integration were demonstrated and noted positively. Tammy asked about modular, user-based pricing and was satisfied with the explanation. The RFI was submitted 14 April per Next_Step__c, so Zycus has formally responded to the documented criteria. The next gate is the full RFP expected fall 2026, where these criteria will be scored and weighted. The criteria are confirmed and fit Zycus's platform directly, with strong module alignment (iSource, iContract, iSupplier, iRisk, ML iAnalyze, Merlin).

ai_fit_signal.summary: No AIS (AI Score) data in Salesforce. Based on call evidence, the buyer is operationally focused and process-driven (manual RFP pain, spend consolidation need, homegrown tool replacement), and they responded positively to the AI demos (autonomous negotiation, contract review, Merlin Teams integration) on the intro call, but AI was not stated as a core requirement or differentiator. The buyer is AI Curious: open to AI capabilities as efficiency enhancers, but not yet anchoring their evaluation around AI-native features. The opportunity is to move them from Curious to Hungry by quantifying the AI impact (cycle-time reduction, cost savings from autonomous negotiation, contract compliance lift from AI review) and positioning Merlin as the wedge against traditional competitors in the fall RFP.

deal_movement.summary: Deal is moving at buyer pace through a structured procurement process with normal long gaps between formal milestones. Intro call 02 March 2026 (116 days ago), RFI issued mid-March, RFI submitted 14 April (73 days ago), pricing call 07 May (50 days ago), Horizon invite sent 20 May (37 days ago), rep outreach 15 June (11 days ago, no buyer response yet). Last verified buyer engagement was the pricing call 07 May. Buyer stated timeline: vendor selection by end of calendar year 2026, RFP expected fall 2026. The 36-day gap since LastActivityDate and the 50-day gap since the last buyer call are longer than ideal but still within the normal enterprise cadence for a deal this early. The rep's 15 June outreach noted 'no updates, project kicks off in the fall,' confirming the buyer's stated fall RFP timeline. The deal is not cold, but engagement is cooling and the next action must re-engage Tammy and Matt before the fall RFP window opens.

confidence_signals.summary: Mixed signals. Positive: buyer is running a structured procurement process (RFI submitted, pricing discussed, RFP expected fall 2026), clear decision criteria and timeline (vendor selection by end of year 2026), strong module fit across all stated requirements, cooperative and engaged evaluators (Tammy and Matt), and continued progression (RFI to pricing to RFP). Cooling: last buyer engagement 07 May (50 days ago), rep outreach 15 June with no response yet (11 days), single-threaded to two mid-level evaluators with no EB or IT/finance engagement, and no competitive intel shared. The 50-day gap is longer than ideal but still within normal enterprise cadence for a deal this early (fall RFP is 2-3 months out). The buyer stated 'project kicks off in the fall' on 15 June, so the silence may be buyer-paced timing, not disengagement. However, the lack of response to the 15 June outreach is a yellow flag. Overall confidence is Medium: the deal is progressing at buyer pace through a real evaluation process, but engagement is cooling and the next 30 days will determine whether Zycus stays top-of-mind or slips into the pack.

north_star_verdict.math: Created 02 March 2026, 116 days in Formal Evaluation stage. Last buyer activity 07 May (pricing call, 50 days ago). Last rep outreach 15 June (11 days ago, no buyer response yet). Buyer stated timeline: vendor selection by end of calendar year 2026, implementation start late 2026 or early 2027. Close date 15 December 2026 (172 days out) sits at the end of their selection window but leaves no room for negotiation, contracting, legal, InfoSec, or any slip in their RFP cycle (RFP expected fall 2026). Path forward requires RFP receipt, evaluation, shortlist, commercials, EB sign-off, MSA/SOW, and InfoSec review, conservatively 4-6 months from RFP issuance. Current pace is buyer-controlled and normal for early-stage enterprise, but forecast date will likely slip into Q1 2027.

best_practice_check.summary: Three substantive win-strategy levers to activate now: (1) map the competitive field and prep differentiation messaging before the fall RFP (unknown competitors = unknown positioning risk), (2) multi-thread beyond Tammy and Matt into IT and finance to build broader sponsorship and uncover approval-chain requirements before the RFP, and (3) quantify the business case (cycle-time savings, Maverick spend reduction, contract compliance lift) to give Tammy and Matt the ROI narrative they need to sell internally and justify budget to the Economic Buyer.

competitive_position.summary: No competitor identified across all sources to date. Tammy stated on 02 March, 'We've talked to a number of suppliers,' confirming a competitive RFI/RFP process, but no vendor names have been shared. This is a multi-vendor evaluation with vendor selection targeted by end of calendar year 2026 and an RFP expected fall 2026. The lack of named competitors is a knowledge gap. Without the competitive field, it is impossible to rank threat level or position Zycus's wedge precisely. The likely field for a Source-to-Pay + Supplier Risk evaluation of this size and scope includes Coupa, SAP Ariba, Jaggaer, and GEP. Zycus's wedge is the unified platform (S2P + embedded Risk, avoiding two RFPs per buyer's stated goal), AI/Merlin capabilities (autonomous negotiation, contract review, spend analysis), deep SAP integration, and modular pricing. The immediate move is to ask Tammy and Matt directly who else is in the running and how they are thinking about the trade-offs, and to run a competitive review with the deal owner's manager to prep differentiation messaging before the fall RFP response.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: AI/Merlin capabilities were demonstrated on the intro call and noted positively by the buyer, but AI is not yet positioned as a primary differentiator or wedge in this deal. Bailey showcased autonomous negotiation agents, contract AI review (risk highlighting, executive summary), and Merlin Teams integration (conversational requisition creation, contract search) on the 02 March call. Tammy and Matt did not explicitly call out AI as a requirement, but they responded positively to the demos and asked clarifying questions. The buyer's stated requirements focus on operational efficiency (manual RFP automation, spend data consolidation, contract tracking) rather than AI-native capabilities, so AI is a 'nice to have' enhancer, not a must-have decision criterion at this stage. However, the fall RFP is an opportunity to position Merlin and AI as the wedge against traditional S2P competitors (Coupa, Ariba) that lack embedded AI depth. The products in scope include Merlin, so the platform supports the AI narrative. The move is to re-position AI as a core differentiator in the RFP response, tying it to the buyer's stated pains (e.g., 'Merlin autonomous negotiation reduces your manual RFP cycle time by 40% and lowers Maverick spend by surfacing non-compliant purchases in real time'), and to provide an AI-focused demo or use case ahead of the RFP to anchor the buyer's evaluation criteria around AI capability, not just module fit.

items.quote: Ideally, we'll find suppliers that have both, and we won't need to do two giant RFPs and implement two tools.
items.criterion: Unified S2P + Supplier Risk platform (avoid two RFPs)

items.quote: We don't have a tool for our RFXs. We do all of that manually.
We have a homegrown tool for document repository... the system basically sends you reminders every 36 to 90 days.
items.criterion: Contract lifecycle with AI review and expiration tracking

items.quote: So for the supplier information, can that data be fed into SAP? ... Duplicate information is very tough to get rid of or get control of.
items.source: Tammy Clark and Bailey Erazo, intro call
items.criterion: Supplier information with SAP integration and deduplication

items.quote: Do you provide something other than that, or do you integrate with our Experian subscription? ... We can integrate with that.
items.source: Tammy Clark and Bailey Erazo, intro call

items.quote: We wanna have all of our data in one place... we're not seeing the leakage, and we're not seeing our total spend with the supplier. ... Can you pull in T and E and credit card data? ... I would imagine so... let me verify.
items.source: Tammy Clark and Bailey Erazo, intro call
items.criterion: Spend analysis with T&E and credit card data

items.quote: We just wanna have a catalog in your system... select it, and then it's gonna ask for approval through SAP. ... Catalogs uploaded in Zycus can be pushed into SAP.
items.source: Matt Palka and Bailey Erazo, intro call
items.criterion: Catalog management with SAP integration (no P2P in Zycus)

items.quote: Pricing questions on 07 May call; Bailey explained modular model and user types (power user vs view/edit), Tammy satisfied.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Strong fit across all stated requirements. Buyer needs unified S2P + supplier risk to avoid two RFPs (Zycus delivers both natively), eSourcing automation (iSource), contract lifecycle with AI review and expiration tracking (iContract + Merlin), supplier information with SAP deduplication and bi-directional sync (iSupplier + SAP integration), risk monitoring with Experian integration (iRisk), spend analysis with T&E and credit card data pull (ML iAnalyze), and catalog management with SAP push (iSupplier + SAP integration). Buyer confirmed scope excludes P2P, invoicing, AP (stays in SAP), and Zycus does not position those modules. Modular pricing fits the buyer's budget constraint (budget for one platform, not two). Tammy and Matt responded positively to the AI capabilities demo (autonomous negotiation agents, contract AI review, Merlin Teams integration) and the SAP integration depth. No misalignment surfaced. The only gap is lack of quantified ROI/value case, which the buyer has not yet built. The fit is strong, and the RFI submission (14 April) and continued engagement (pricing call, RFP template request, Horizon invite) confirm Zycus is advancing.

hard.ais_why:
• Intake is in scope as part of S2C with requisitions, but it is framed as scope rather than buyer-voice AI excitement.
• Description is empty and there are no Avoma calls. Engagement is RFI/RFP-driven, with the RFP expected back in the fall.
• Qualified, $120K. Vendor selection by EOY 2026 and 2027 implementation. No urgency or AI mandate signaled.

deltas.subject: Single-threaded to procurement evaluators: only Tammy (Category Manager) and Matt (Manager, Indirect Procurement) engaged to date. No IT stakeholder (despite SAP integration scope), no finance (despite budget and ROI implications), no senior procurement leadership (Mandy Felix, Melissa Cameron), no Economic Buyer. For a $200k platform decision with year-end selection goal, multi-threading into IT and finance is essential to uncover approval requirements, build broader sponsorship, and reduce ghost risk. Move: ask Tammy and Matt for intros to IT lead and finance lead before the RFP.

deltas.subject: No quantified business case: buyer articulated clear operational pain (manual RFPs, fragmented spend data, homegrown tool limitations, Maverick spend leakage) but no dollar savings target, cycle-time reduction, or ROI.
